summary: A symmetrical, single-story pavilion with a cruciform raised red-brick roof set inside a rectangular lot. Four tall corner towers (one at each corner) rise above the flat ground and have overhanging tiled caps. The central roof is cross-shaped with stepped brick slopes and stone trim; low layered walls of light sandstone and white stone surround a raised interior platform. A short white stone block border marks the plot edge on packed-dirt ground.

shape.overall_form: central cruciform roof sitting on a rectangular base with four corner towers
shape.symmetry: bilaterally symmetrical along both axes (approximate fourfold symmetry)

elements: four tall rectangular towers with overhanging caps
low layered perimeter walls with alternating light bands
rebuild_hints: Establish a rectangular plot and lay a single-block-high white border (spaced blocks visible) before building interior.

Construct four identical corner towers; towers are ~11-13 blocks tall with vertical light sandstone faces and stonebrick/quartz corner trim; add small recessed glass window blocks at regular intervals.
Create a cruciform roof with red brick blocks in stepped slopes from a central ridge; trim edges with stone slabs and a recessed stone/brick band under the slab overhang.
Build low surrounding walls using alternating horizontal bands: light sandstone banding separated by white stone/quartz layers and vertical white corner pilasters that align with towers.
uncertainties: Exact block types for the white trim may be quartz or smooth stone (labeled as quartz above with moderate confidence).
The light yellow band appears to be sandstone but could be a variant (smooth sandstone vs regular sandstone) — sandstone chosen with high but not total confidence.
The small light sources on top of roof caps look like sea lanterns but could be beacons or glowstone; identification uncertain.
Interior floor height and any internal partitions are not visible; estimate assumes a single open interior platform.
Precise footprint dimensions measured in blocks are estimated from bird's-eye views and may be off by several blocks.
